summary: Gruber dissects John Battelle's defense of Google after the Wall Street Journal revealed Google was circumventing Safari's default cookie privacy settings to enable third-party tracking. Battelle argued that Apple's default settings were the real problem, since users never explicitly chose them, and that Google was merely following standard web practices. Gruber methodically dismantles this argument, noting that default settings matter precisely because most users never change them, and that Google deliberately wrote code to bypass a browser's privacy protections — not merely followed industry norms. He detects a sense of entitlement in the ad industry's reaction: because tracking was once near-universal, they believe they're entitled to continue it regardless of browser defaults. Gruber draws a parallel to Flash and pop-up blocking, arguing Safari isn't breaking the open web but simply disrupting comfortable assumptions.
key_points: Default settings are critically important because most users never change them, making the choice of default a substantive privacy decision
Google deliberately wrote code to circumvent Safari's cookie blocking — this wasn't an accident or a gray area, it was an active bypass of user privacy settings
Battelle's defense conflates Google following 'standard practice' with Google circumventing a browser setting designed to block that practice
The ad industry's reaction reveals a sense of entitlement: past ability to track users is treated as a permanent right
Safari's default mirrors what most users would likely choose if they understood how third-party cookies work
money_quotes: Most users don't change settings and just use the defaults. Default settings are incredibly important.

The difference with Safari isn't that Apple has made an assumption about the user's view regarding cookie privacy; it's that Apple has made a different assumption than that made by other browser vendors.
Safari hasn't broken the web; it has simply broken the heretofore safe assumption that an overwhelming majority of web surfers accepted third-party cookies.
What's being criticized is Google devising and implementing a method to store third-party cookies in web browsers which are set not to accept third-party cookies.
What I detect in Google's behavior (and Battelle's more-or-less defense of it) is a sense of entitlement.

Arguing that Google didn't do anything wrong — or all that wrong — is one thing. But trying to spin this into an argument that Apple has done something wrong, and that Google was just reacting naturally, is something else.
So Safari isn't part of the 'open web' because it doesn't allow ad networks to track users across websites by default?
key_insight: Google circumventing Safari's cookie privacy defaults wasn't standard web practice — it was an act of entitlement by an ad industry that conflates its historical ability to track users with a right to do so.
